statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, sort it in ascending order using the Insertion Sort algorithm. The algorithm works by iterating through the array one element at a time, inserting each element into its proper position within the previously sorted portion of the array.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The sorted array, with each element on a new line.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array.

examples.explanation: The array is sorted in ascending order using Insertion Sort.
The array is sorted in ascending order using Insertion Sort.

hints.text: The problem requires sorting an array in ascending order. The Insertion Sort algorithm is a simple sorting algorithm that works by dividing the input into a sorted and an unsorted region.

The algorithm starts with the first element as a sorted array of size 1. Then, it iterates through the rest of the array, inserting each element into its correct position within the sorted portion of the array.

To insert an element into its correct position, compare it with the elements in the sorted portion and shift those elements one position to the right until the correct position for the new element is found.
The core technique here involves iterating through the array and for each element, shifting previous elements to make room for it if necessary, effectively maintaining a sorted subarray.

editorial.title: Insertion Sort: A Simple yet Efficient Sorting Algorithm
editorial.intuition: The problem requires sorting an array of integers in ascending order. The intuition behind Insertion Sort is to iterate through the array one element at a time, inserting each element into its proper position within the previously sorted portion of the array.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve comparing each pair of elements and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. This would result in a time complexity of O(n^2) but with a higher constant factor due to the increased number of comparisons and swaps. Insertion Sort improves upon this by only making comparisons and swaps as necessary to insert each element into its proper position.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is to use Insertion Sort, which has a time complexity of O(n^2) and a space complexity of O(1).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is Insertion Sort itself. It works by iterating through the array one element at a time, inserting each element into its proper position within the previously sorted portion of the array. This is achieved by shifting larger elements to the right until the correct position for the current element is found.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of Insertion Sort can be proven by induction. The base case is when the array has only one element, which is trivially sorted. For the inductive step, assume that the first i elements are sorted. When the (i+1)th element is inserted, it is compared with the previous elements and shifted to its correct position, ensuring that the first i+1 elements are sorted. Therefore, by induction, the entire array is sorted after all elements have been inserted.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with an example array [5, 2, 8, 3, 1] illustrates the process: Start with the first element [5], then insert 2 to get [2, 5], insert 8 to get [2, 5, 8], insert 3 to get [2, 3, 5, 8], and finally insert 1 to get [1, 2, 3, 5, 8].
commonMistakes: Not recognizing that Insertion Sort has a best-case time complexity of O(n) when the input array is already sorted.
Assuming that Insertion Sort is only suitable for small arrays and not for larger datasets.
Failing to realize that Insertion Sort can be used as a building block for more complex sorting algorithms.
Not understanding the stability of Insertion Sort, which preserves the order of equal elements.
Overlooking the simplicity and efficiency of Insertion Sort for nearly sorted data.
Confusing Insertion Sort with other sorting algorithms like Selection Sort or Bubble Sort.
